name: Drip pricing
summary: The advertised price is lower than the all-in price at checkout because mandatory fees (service, resort, cleaning, delivery, processing) are disclosed only progressively as the user advances through the flow.

evidence.claim: FTC 2024 'Junk Fees' Rule (16 CFR Part 464) requires upfront all-in pricing for short-term lodging and live-event tickets; prohibits deceptive drip-pricing patterns.
FTC 2022 'Bringing Dark Patterns to Light' report categorizes drip pricing as a deceptive pricing pattern under Section 5 of the FTC Act.
California AB-537 'Honest Pricing Law' (effective July 2024) requires all mandatory fees to appear in the advertised price statewide.

body.description: The headline price shown in search results or ads is lower than the all-in price the user actually pays. Mandatory fees (service fee, resort fee, cleaning fee, order-processing fee, booking fee, convenience fee) are revealed one at a time as the user clicks deeper into the purchase flow, after psychological commitment has formed.

detectionHeuristics.description: Compare the price shown on the listing/search page with the final total at the checkout confirmation step. Flag if delta exceeds 10%.
Presence of these fee labels at a late checkout step (not on the listing page) is a strong drip-pricing signal.
New mandatory fees appearing AFTER the user enters contact/payment information.

body.llmVerifyPrompt: Compare the advertised price (listing page, ad, headline) with the total charged at the final confirmation step. Are all mandatory non-optional fees disclosed in the first price, or only progressively? If progressive, this is drip pricing — flag the category of fee and the jurisdiction.
body.remediation: Surface all mandatory fees on the listing page in the Lens overlay. Recommend the user compare the all-in total, not the advertised price. For lodging and event tickets, cite FTC Junk Fees Rule (E1) and advise reporting to the FTC.
